title: What's WRONG With Your Milk (and how to fix it)

chunks.title: Introduction to Dairy Types
chunks.summary: The segment introduces the differences between various types of dairy, specifically focusing on the variants of casein protein (A1 vs. A2) found in milk. It emphasizes the potential health implications of consuming A1 milk as opposed to A2 and discusses how to choose dairy options for better nutrition.
chunks.transcript: You're a fan of raw dairy. You mentioned starting the day with goat milk. But let's really get into the details here. We have the A1 versus A2, pasteurization which you've touched on, but let's go deeper into that, homogenization. Different animals, you know, we got the sheep milk, goat milk, cow milk. For somebody that's just been very conventional when it comes to dairy and having say a homogenized pasteurized A1 cow dairy. Let's walk through the different steps here and how they can upgrade.

chunks.title: Health Benefits of Dairy Fat
chunks.summary: This section highlights the health benefits of dairy fat, particularly focusing on odd-chain fatty acids like C15 and C17 that can protect against certain cell death processes. It discusses how full-fat dairy can be a source of these beneficial compounds and how they relate to overall human health.

If you're talking about cows, Jersey cows and Guernsey cows are all A2. But most other cows are going to be some mixture of A1 and A2. chunks.title: Risks and Safety of Raw Milk
chunks.summary: The discussion turns to the risks associated with raw milk versus pasteurized milk, emphasizing the importance of sourcing it from reputable farmers. It outlines the potential contamination issues of both raw and pasteurized milk, providing a historical context for concerns surrounding raw milk consumption.
chunks.transcript: All goat milk, all sheep milk, all bison milk is all A2. Anything but cow's milk is all A2. What I drank growing up was probably A1 skim pasteurized homogenized milk. Dairy fat is super healthy for humans. It's so incredibly healthy for humans. There's a compound in dairy fat called C15 and C17. These are odd-chain fatty acids and recently I read a study showing that levels of C15, which is called pentadecanoic acid, correlate with protection from lipid peroxide induced programmed death, which is called ferroptosis. So, people may have heard the term apoptosis, but ferroptosis is what happens when you have too much oxidative stress in your body related to too many polyunsaturated fatty acids, harkening back to our previous conversation. One of the challenges with polyunsaturated fatty acids is their oxidative liability. And it can lead to programmed cell death, which is called ferroptosis. Not good. That's not a good thing. C15 protects against that. Pentadecanoic acid. So, a fat found in full-fat dairy, something that is widely criticized and said to cause heart disease, actually protects against lipid peroxide induced cell death in humans. The quality with which your farmer makes his or her raw milk is everything. And so, you want to get it from a farmer that you trust that is raising cows in a clean way, that is cleaning the udders, and is doing it in a sanitary way. Pasteurized milk also can become contaminated with E. coli and salmonella and campylobacter. The one of the largest outbreaks ever in our history in America was 1986. chunks.title: Nutritional Benefits of Raw Milk
chunks.summary: This segment presents the unique benefits of raw milk, including its possible immunological advantages and its impact on childhood allergies. The speaker shares personal experiences and anecdotal evidence supporting the consumption of raw milk and its benefits compared to pasteurized options.
chunks.transcript: outcompete any listeria, any E. coli, any campylobacter, any salmonella. Now, every once in a while raw milk does get contaminated and people get sick. chunks.title: Calcium and Nutrient Balancing
chunks.summary: Focusing on the importance of calcium and phosphorus balance in diets, this section emphasizes the role of dairy in achieving nutritional balance. It discusses how dairy provides essential nutrients that might not be as easily obtainable from other sources.
chunks.transcript: diet. I don't think everyone needs to do it, but I think it's a beautiful, delicious thing that adds a lot of variety and unique nutrients to my diet. And if you're not thinking about a calcium-phosphorus balance in your diet, you're leaving you're leaving money on the table in terms of your overall health. You cannot just eat meat without balancing it with calcium. Calcium-phosphorus balance is important, just like zinc and copper balance is important. And we can talk about that,

chunks.title: Homogenization Process and Preferences
chunks.summary: The final part covers the homogenization process of milk, discussing its implications for texture and flavor. It emphasizes a preference for unhomogenized milk and the sensory pleasure of cream rising to the top, while mentioning the ongoing debate about the health effects of homogenized versus non-homogenized milk.
